posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
Not unexpectedly, give a 2013 vintage to Dunn, and he produces a monster. This is one of the more dense, structured, but still rich Dunns that I have tried. Now mind you, if I owned it I would hide it for 15+ years for starters, but it was still fun, in a scientific sort of way, working through this bottle over the course of a several hour dinner. A buddy also brought and we popped a 2012 Turley Hayne’s Vineyard Petite Sirah, which was terribly outclassed.

The Dunn is pitch black with tinges of dark purple. Just its appearance is brooding, impenetrable. Reticent nose to start, but it’s the structure on the palate that makes an impression. Dense layers of the red, blue, black fruit spectrum, wet earth, tar, coffee roast, all held in place by massive, dusty tannins, lifted - if that even seems possible with its weight - by very vibrant acid. It’s the acid that somehow makes this weight and youth work. Long chewy finish with some sweetness to the tannins and a tinge of bitter green on the edges that I enjoyed. I do not recall Dunn’s oak regime, but even with the scale and ripeness of this wine, the alcohol and wood remained in complete harmony and balance. I could not say that about the Turley.

This Dunn is built for the super long haul, think like 25+ years.

(94++ pts.)

posts.name: T 0 m Altmayer
posts.cooked: Nice note, thanks. When I read notes like this, I’m makes me sad that i don’t have more from Dunn. I used to really enjoy the “regular” bottling, but the Howell Mountain was always something special. Glad to hear they are still rolling along.
I had a 1999 the other night that was quite good.

posts.name: Chris Seiber
posts.cooked: Alan Eden was right, these 2013s just haven’t matured into being good drinking.

posts.name: Anton Dotson
posts.cooked:
Synchronicity!

Last Friday, we opened an '88, '93, '98, '03, '08, and a '13.

All were nice.

The 88 was a classically Dunn back-boned, lean California cab, plenty of aging room left. Cedar/tobacco, subdued fruit. Loooong finish. Still the 'old style.

93 was much fruitier, but in the family. It was a crowd pleaser.

98 seemed to represent a small shift in style, more vanilla/oak, brighter berries. Not that it was simply more youthful, seemed to be more fruit forward…but not to a crazy extent. This is Dunn, after-all.

'03 was along those lines, maybe more evident oak. Well balanced at what I consider young for this winery.

'08 was similar to 03.

13 was like you said. It’s going to be a fun trip. It seems to have the most harmony between fruit and wood of the 03, 08, and it.

What a great ‘lifetime’ winery.

posts.name: Robert Alfert
posts.cooked:
What I killer line-up, Anton.

The 91-93 Dunns are some of he finest Cali Cabs that I’ve had, some tried recently were just gorgeous.

posts.name: Justin S ! n g h
posts.cooked:
Does 2014 mark a stylistic change for Dunn, or is it perhaps just a hallmark of the vintage? There was an article a few years back on changes Mike Dunn wished to implement, presumably to make the wines more approachable in their youth, and I assume those changes have been implemented over time. The professional reviews on the 2014 below touch on this, but I don’t recall the same reviewers using adjectives like “softer” and “restrained” with respect to the 2013.

96 points Decanter
Notes of black cherry, cassis, graphite, incense, spice and rich soil preface an authoritative, intense wine with a lovely chassis of fine but firm tannins, underpinned by vibrant acidity: quintessential Dunn Howell Mountain. Under Mike Dunn’s thoughtful stewardship, the family’s wines have become more refined; largely thanks to more carefully chosen barrels. But while this wine may be less forbidding in its youth than the vintages of the 1990s, let it sleep for a decade to reveal its potential complexity. (05/2017)

91-94 points Robert Parker’s Wine Advocate
The 2014 Cabernet Sauvignon Howell Mountain is still in barrel and looks to be a 25- to 35-year wine. It is much softer than one normally expects from the old classic vintages of Randy Dunn, but the son seems to be trying to build more approachability into the wines without sacrificing any of their sheer concentration and extract. Blue and black fruits, earth, truffle notes, camphor and creosote are all present in this big, juicy, succulent mountain Cabernet Sauvignon that should be approachable young, hit its peak in 10-15 years and last 25-30. (RP) (10/2016)

93 points Vinous
Inky blue/purplish fruit, graphite, lavender, grilled herbs and smoke run through the 2014 Cabernet Sauvignon (Howell Mountain). The 2014 has all the Dunn signatures in its flavor profile, but in the mid-weight style of the year. This is an especially restrained, young wine for Dunn with far less of the tannic heft that readers familiar with these wines are likely to expect. (AG) (3/2018)
I’ve had the 1985 Howell Mountain on three occasion in the last year and it is an incredible wine. Only just entering secondary development. Easily my wine of 2017 and probably 2018, too.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
Justin S:

Does 2014 mark a stylistic change for Dunn, or is it perhaps just a hallmark of the vintage? There was an article a few years back on changes Mike Dunn wished to implement, presumably to make the wines more approachable in their youth, and I assume those changes have been implemented over time. The professional reviews on the 2014 below touch on this, but I don’t recall the same reviewers using adjectives like “softer” and “restrained” with respect to the 2013.

96 points Decanter
Notes of black cherry, cassis, graphite, incense, spice and rich soil preface an authoritative, intense wine with a lovely chassis of fine but firm tannins, underpinned by vibrant acidity: quintessential Dunn Howell Mountain. Under Mike Dunn’s thoughtful stewardship, the family’s wines have become more refined; largely thanks to more carefully chosen barrels. But while this wine may be less forbidding in its youth than the vintages of the 1990s, let it sleep for a decade to reveal its potential complexity. (05/2017)

91-94 points Robert Parker’s Wine Advocate
The 2014 Cabernet Sauvignon Howell Mountain is still in barrel and looks to be a 25- to 35-year wine. It is much softer than one normally expects from the old classic vintages of Randy Dunn, but the son seems to be trying to build more approachability into the wines without sacrificing any of their sheer concentration and extract. Blue and black fruits, earth, truffle notes, camphor and creosote are all present in this big, juicy, succulent mountain Cabernet Sauvignon that should be approachable young, hit its peak in 10-15 years and last 25-30. (RP) (10/2016)

93 points Vinous
Inky blue/purplish fruit, graphite, lavender, grilled herbs and smoke run through the 2014 Cabernet Sauvignon (Howell Mountain). The 2014 has all the Dunn signatures in its flavor profile, but in the mid-weight style of the year. This is an especially restrained, young wine for Dunn with far less of the tannic heft that readers familiar with these wines are likely to expect. (AG) (3/2018)

In a sense, but the article you’re referring to (and several other commentators) largely got it wrong. In brief: over the years, Randy, who still called all the picks up until last year, had been picking a bit later, and then opting to remove alcohol if the wine exceeded 14%. Mike Dunn wants to pick at the sort of maturity that Randy picked at back in the 1990s, when he produced wines with natural alcohol in the mid to high 13s. Simultaneously, Mike has made some changes to the élevage regime: Randy used to use 50% new oak from Vicard, with wide grain, for as much oak impact for as little expenditure as possible. In pursuit of less aggressive wood tannin and in an attempt (which since 2003 or so has succeeded) to eliminate the brettanomyces which invaded the cellar in the late 1990s, Mike has gone over to using 100% new oak but selects barrels that are very low impact. He runs numerous barrel trials and really works on tailoring the cooperage to the wine.

Nothing about the wines’ concentration, fruit tannin or aging potential has changed. They just carry less coarse oak tannin, less brett and—in the future—we can expect them to carry a touch less natural alcohol.

Unfortunately, several commentators have misinterpreted this as Mike trying to make ‘softer’ more commercial wines, which couldn’t be further from the truth.

posts.name: larry schaffer
posts.cooked:
William,

Great insights - thanks for sharing. Any idea of how often the wines are racked during elevage? I wonder if perhaps they are increasing that number to make the wines more ‘user friendly’ early? Just a thought . . .

Cheers.

posts.name: William Kelley
posts.cooked:
larry schaffer:

William,

Great insights - thanks for sharing. Any idea of how often the wines are racked during elevage? I wonder if perhaps they are increasing that number to make the wines more ‘user friendly’ early? Just a thought . . .

Cheers.

Mike writes:

“We have always racked every 6 months and the most current vintage gets racked after ML completion so three times the first year. We clear out the entry hall after bottling by racking the current vintage back into a tunnel lane….”

He also adds that greater oxygen uptake from new barrels is probably helping to soften the vintage a bit.

posts.cooked: Very helpful William. Thanks.
Sounds like I’ll need a 2017 of this wine as an anniversary wine and age out a few throughout the decades! And of course I’ll need a 2013, for science
